response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Look, I'm not on social media much, maybe a few times a week when I have downtime from my cleaning business, and honestly that's probably a good thing given all the garbage floating around out there. Fake content drives me up the wall because you can't trust anything anymore. I see these posts about miracle cleaning products or some political nonsense that gets people riled up, and half the time it's complete fiction designed to manipulate you. It's exhausting trying to figure out what's real and what isn't, and frankly it adds to the stress I already deal with on a daily basis. My approach has become pretty straightforward and maybe a bit harsh, but it works for me. If something looks too good to be true or designed to make me angry, I just scroll past it or get off the platform entirely. I don't have the energy to fact-check every single thing, and I'm certainly not going to argue with strangers online about what's real. That's a waste of time I could spend with my kid or out rock climbing where things are actually solid and real. When I do see something that seems important, I'll maybe check a couple of different sources before I believe it, but I'm skeptical by nature anyway. The platforms themselves don't seem to care much about fixing the problem because controversy keeps people engaged, which is pretty cynical if you ask me. I've told my child to be careful about what they believe online and to come talk to me if something seems off. Teaching critical thinking seems more important than trying to police every fake post out there. At the end of the day, limiting my time on these platforms has been the most effective strategy. The less I'm exposed to it, the less it affects my mental state, and I'm already dealing with enough without adding digital deception to the mix. Real life has enough challenges without inventing new ones online.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 48-year-old self-employed cleaner with high neuroticism (83) and low extraversion (10) and agreeableness (26), who is also dealing with depression. This suggests someone who is introverted, somewhat cynical, easily stressed, and not particularly trusting or warm toward others. They spend limited time on social media (few times weekly), which would inform their practical, avoidance-based approach. Their moderate openness (65) suggests they can consider different perspectives but aren't naive. The low agreeableness indicates they might be more blunt and less concerned with social niceties. Their occupation as a self-employed cleaner and upper secondary education suggests practical, no-nonsense thinking rather than academic analysis. Having one child means they'd consider protecting younger people from misinformation. The depression and high neuroticism would make overwhelming information particularly stressful, leading to coping strategies focused on avoidance and limitation. The tone should be somewhat weary, skeptical, practical, and direct without being overly friendly or optimistic.
response_parsed.tone: skeptical, weary, direct, practical, somewhat cynical
